title: Controllability-Guided Flow Matching for Online MBRL (CtrlFlow + CGLC)
abstract: Replace step-wise dynamics modeling in online model-based RL with a conditional flow-matching trajectory generator that directly samples full trajectories from initial states. Augment the conditional flow-matching generator with a Controllability-Guided Latent Conditioner (CGLC) which injects a minimal-control-energy scalar—computed from a low-rank empirical nonlinear controllability Gramian approximation—into the trajectory generator and as an attention bias. Train the flow-matching model with a combined loss: flow-matching likelihood, a minimum-control-energy regularizer (L_control) derived from the Gramian, and a value-guidance term to bias sampling toward high-return trajectories. The design preserves full-trajectory generation (no step-wise dynamics predictors), and targets improved ...
introduction:
Controllability-Guided Flow Matching for Online MBRL (CtrlFlow + CGLC) builds on recent literature to tackle the current topic. Replace step-wise dynamics modeling in online model-based RL with a conditional flow-matching trajectory generator that directly samples full trajectories from initial states. Augment the conditional flow-matching generator with a Controllability-Guided Latent Conditioner (CGLC) which injects a minimal-control-energy scalar—computed from a low-rank empirical nonlinear controllability Gramian approximation—into the trajectory generator and as an attention bias. Train the flow-matching model with a combined loss: flow-matching likelihood, a minimum-control-energy regularizer (L_control) derived from the Gramian, and a value-guidance term to bias sampling toward high-return trajectories. The design preserves full-trajectory generation (no step-wise dynamics predictors), and targets improved ...

Key references informing this idea:
- CHD: Coupled Hierarchical Diffusion for Long-Horizon Tasks: The paper introduces Coupled Hierarchical Diffusion (CHD), a new framework aimed at improving long-horizon planning through joint generation of high-level subgoals and low-level trajectories via a unified diffusion process. It addresses shortcomings in existing hierarchical diffusion planners relating to the independence of HL and LL planning, enhancing trajectory coherence and overall performance.

- Policy Representation via Diffusion Probability Model for Reinforcement Learning: The paper addresses the limitations of traditional reinforcement learning algorithms that produce unimodal policy distributions, which can hinder exploration and expressiveness in complex environments. It introduces DIPO, a novel reinforcement learning algorithm that utilizes diffusion probability models to represent policies as stochastic processes, ensuring multimodal capabilities. Theoretical foundations and practical implementations are provided, highlighting the convergence of diffusion policy representation and its effectiveness in various continuous control tasks in the Mujoco benchmarks.
- Diffusion-based Reinforcement Learning via Q-weighted Variational Policy Optimization: The study presents a novel model-free online reinforcement learning algorithm named Q-weighted Variational Policy Optimization (QVPO) that integrates diffusion policies into online settings. It introduces a Q-weighted variational loss that serves as a tight lower bound of the policy objective, enhancing exploration and reducing sample inefficiency.
- Push Smarter, Not Harder: Hierarchical RL-Diffusion Policy for Efficient Nonprehensile Manipulation: {"key_contributions": ["Introduction of HeRD (Hierarchical Reinforcement Learning-Diffusion Policy), which combines high-level goal selection with low-level trajectory generation for nonprehensile manipulation.", "Proposal of a goal-conditioned diffusion policy framework that generates collision-free trajectories based on human demonstrations.", "Demonstration of HeRD's effectiveness across multiple environments, outperforming the state-of-the-art Spatial Action Maps (SAM) in success rates and e
- Large Scale Pursuit-Evasion Under Collision Avoidance Using Deep Reinforcement Learning: This paper addresses large-scale pursuit-evasion games with decentralized deep reinforcement learning agents using a Mix-Attention state processing method, enabling scalable and robust multi-agent coordination under kinematic constraints.
- Exploring the limits of hierarchical world models in reinforcement learning: The authors propose a hierarchical model-based reinforcement learning framework with static, environment-agnostic temporal abstraction, allowing concurrent multi-level agent training but facing challenges in model exploitation at abstract levels.
- Deep Reinforcement Learning from Self-Play in Imperfect-Information Games: This work introduces Neural Fictitious Self-Play (NFSP), combining fictitious self-play and deep reinforcement learning to approach Nash equilibria in imperfect-information games, achieving near state-of-the-art performance in poker domains.
- Game of Drones: Multi-UAV Pursuit-Evasion Game With Online Motion Planning by Deep Reinforcement Learning: The study investigates multi-UAV pursuit-evasion games in obstacle-rich environments using a novel multiagent deep RL framework with target prediction, resulting in improved pursuit performance and robustness in damaged swarm scenarios.
- Adversarial Search and Tracking with Multiagent Reinforcement Learning in Sparsely Observable Environment: This paper tackles adversarial search and tracking with limited observations by proposing a multi-agent RL framework with a learnable filtering model, significantly increasing detection rates in large, sparsely observable environments.
- STLGame: Signal Temporal Logic Games in Adversarial Multi-Agent Systems: The authors present STLGame, a framework for robust policy synthesis in adversarial multi-agent systems using signal temporal logic and fictitious self-play, achieving nearly unexploitable policies in continuous control benchmarks.

algorithm.name: Controllability-Guided Flow-Matching Trajectory Generator (CtrlFlow+CGLC)
input: Initial state s0 (from environment or replay buffer)
Interaction-limited online RL environment (e.g., Cartpole, Humanoid)
Online dataset of state-action trajectories (collected during RL)
Hyperparameters: trajectory length T, Gramian window k, loss weights (lambda_ctrl, lambda_val)
output: Sampled full state-action trajectories tau = (s0, a0, s1, a1, ..., sT, aT)
Predicted cumulative reward for each trajectory
pipeline: Step 1: Embed the initial state s0 using a neural encoder to obtain state_embedding.
Step 2: Compute a low-rank empirical nonlinear controllability Gramian over a window of k steps using recent online trajectories and a local linearization head.
Step 3: Pass state_embedding through the Controllability-Guided Latent Conditioner (CGLC), a small MLP, to predict a normalized log-minimum-control-energy scalar.

Step 4: Concatenate the CGLC scalar to the initial-state conditioning vector and inject it as a soft additive attention bias at each denoising timestep in the conditional flow-matching trajectory generator.

Step 5: Sample a noise trajectory and iteratively denoise it using the conditional flow-matching model, conditioned on the initial state and CGLC scalar, to generate a full state-action trajectory tau of length T.
Step 6: For each generated trajectory, compute the predicted cumulative reward using the reward function and value estimator.
Step 7: Compute the minimum-control-energy regularizer L_control for the trajectory using the Gramian approximation.
Step 8: Compute the value-guidance loss L_value for the trajectory using the value estimator.
Step 9: Train the flow-matching model and CGLC jointly using the combined loss: flow-matching likelihood + lambda_ctrl * L_control + lambda_val * L_value.
Step 10: Periodically update the empirical Gramian and CGLC parameters using new online data.
Step 11: Output sampled trajectories, predicted rewards, control-energy statistics, and updated model parameters.

reference_papers.title: CHD: Coupled Hierarchical Diffusion for Long-Horizon Tasks
reference_papers.authors: Ce Hao, Anxing Xiao, Zhiwei Xue, Harold Soh
reference_papers.summary: CHD introduces a unified diffusion process that tightly couples high-level sub-goal selection and low-level trajectory generation, improving coherence and performance in long-horizon planning tasks. The framework leverages classifier feedback to self-correct sub-goals during sampling, outperforming flat and hierarchical baselines.
reference_papers.contribution: This paper demonstrates the effectiveness of trajectory-level diffusion models for coherent long-horizon planning, supporting the idea of replacing step-wise dynamics with full-trajectory generators as in CtrlFlow. Its hierarchical coupling inspires the controllability-guided conditioning and attention bias mechanisms proposed in CGLC.

reference_papers.title: Planning with Diffusion for Flexible Behavior Synthesis
reference_papers.authors: Michael Janner, Yilun Du, J. Tenenbaum, S. Levine
reference_papers.summary: This work folds trajectory optimization into the modeling process using diffusion probabilistic models, enabling planning by iterative denoising of trajectories. The approach allows classifier-guided sampling and demonstrates strong performance in flexible, long-horizon control tasks.
reference_papers.contribution: The paper provides foundational evidence that diffusion models can directly generate full trajectories for planning, validating the core premise of CtrlFlow. Its classifier-guided sampling motivates the value-guidance and control-energy regularization terms in the proposed algorithm.

reference_papers.title: Policy Representation via Diffusion Probability Model for Reinforcement Learning
reference_papers.authors: Long Yang, Zhixiong Huang, Fenghao Lei, Yucun Zhong, Yiming Yang, Cong Fang, Shiting Wen, Binbin Zhou, Zhouchen Lin
reference_papers.summary: This paper establishes a theoretical foundation for representing RL policies via diffusion probability models, proving convergence and demonstrating multimodal policy expressiveness. The DIPO algorithm applies diffusion policy to online model-free RL, showing empirical superiority on Mujoco benchmarks.
reference_papers.contribution: By formalizing diffusion-based policy representation and showing its effectiveness in online RL, this work supports the feasibility of training trajectory-level diffusion models in online settings, as required by CtrlFlow. Its multimodal policy insights inform the design of the controllability-guided conditioner for diverse trajectory sampling.

reference_papers.title: Diffusion-based Reinforcement Learning via Q-weighted Variational Policy Optimization
reference_papers.authors: Shutong Ding, Ke Hu, Zhenhao Zhang, Kan Ren, Weinan Zhang, Jingyi Yu, Jingya Wang, Ye Shi
reference_papers.summary: QVPO is a model-free diffusion-based online RL algorithm that introduces a Q-weighted variational loss, providing a tight lower bound for policy objectives and enhancing exploration through entropy regularization. The method achieves state-of-the-art results in cumulative reward and sample efficiency on MuJoCo tasks.
reference_papers.contribution: This paper demonstrates that diffusion models can be effectively trained in online RL with value-guided objectives, directly supporting the value-guidance term in CtrlFlow. Its approach to policy improvement and sample efficiency informs the combined loss and online training pipeline of the proposed algorithm.

reference_papers.title: Push Smarter, Not Harder: Hierarchical RL-Diffusion Policy for Efficient Nonprehensile Manipulation
reference_papers.summary: HeRD combines hierarchical RL for high-level goal selection with low-level goal-conditioned diffusion models for trajectory generation, enabling efficient and generalizable nonprehensile manipulation. The method outperforms baselines in success rate, path efficiency, and adaptability.
reference_papers.contribution: This work validates the use of hierarchical and goal-conditioned diffusion models for trajectory generation in RL, supporting the integration of controllability-guided conditioning and attention bias in CtrlFlow+CGLC. Its results reinforce the scalability and efficiency of generative trajectory planning architectures.

baselines.name: CCLF: A Contrastive-Curiosity-Driven Learning Framework for Sample-Efficient Reinforcement Learning
baselines.usage: CCLF introduces a model-agnostic framework that prioritizes experience replay, selects the most informative augmented inputs, and regularizes the Q-function and encoder to focus on under-learned data using contrastive curiosity, significantly reducing the need for augmented inputs.

baselines.name: Meta-Reinforcement Learning Robust to Distributional Shift Via Performing Lifelong In-Context Learning
baselines.usage: Proposes PSBL, which meta-trains a transformer to perform amortized inference of the Predictive Posterior Distribution (PPD) of the optimal policy. It enables lifelong in-context learning with frozen parameters, sampling actions from the PPD to progressively reduce uncertainty during online interaction.

baselines.name: Constrained Reinforcement Learning for Dexterous Manipulation
baselines.usage: We investigate adding position-based constraints (specifically a cylindrical boundary constraint) to a 24-DOF robot hand task using Constrained Policy Optimization (CPO). This ensures the robot learns to move towards the object sooner and maintains safe behavior during training and rollouts without sacrificing sample efficiency compared to unconstrained methods.

evaluation.fairness_protocol: Good start: same online interaction budget (E1) and explicit baselines (E3) are stated, plus ablations (remove CGLC, lambda_ctrl=0, identity-Gramian). Missing controls: (1) normalize by wall-clock or number of gradient updates since added SVD increases compute; (2) compare against a similarly-parameterized conditioner that uses simple proxies (e.g., ||a||^2 or learned energy predictor without Gramian) to show the Gramian-specific benefit; (3) ensure identical trajectory length T, sampling steps, and planner call frequency at evaluation time across methods; (4) include at least one stochastic/partial-observation game-like benchmark (not only MuJoCo) to match the stated 'games' motivation.
evaluation.feedback: Mechanism-commit-innovation is substantive (CGLC + low-rank empirical Gramian) and does address the stated defect 'unclear_controllability_integration', but the ICML bar will hinge on (a) demonstrating that controllability guidance improves long-horizon coherence beyond what generic action-penalties/energy-regularization already give, and (b) showing robustness in game-like stochastic/adversarial settings rather than only standard control. Tighten the method by specifying exactly what L_control is (e.g., u^T W^{-1} u surrogate; how u is defined for a trajectory sample), how the linearization head is trained/updated online, and how confidence-based fallback is triggered (singular value gap threshold). Add a proxy-baseline: replace Gramian with (i) simple action-norm cost, (ii) learned 'dif...
evaluation.defect_fix_summary: Addresses defects ['long-horizon_model_error_compounding', 'unclear_controllability_integration', 'validation_gap'] by operationalizing controllability (M2) via a low-rank empirical Gramian and injecting it through CGLC into the flow-matching generator, plus adding targeted ablations/stress-tests to validate whether controllability guidance (not just extra conditioning) improves long-horizon robustness under online MBRL budgets.

iterations.rationale: Add a Controllability-Guided Latent Conditioner (CGLC): a small neural module that computes a minimal-control-energy score from an approximate nonlinear controllability Gramian and injects it into the conditional flow-matching network as a scalar conditioning feature and attention bias during trajectory generation. The CGLC is trained jointly with the flow-matching loss using an explicit minimum-control-energy regularizer (L_control) that penalizes sampled trajectories requiring high control energy, weighted against the value-guidance term.

Jacobians via autodiff? finite differences?) and how MCE(τ) is computed for stochastic dynamics. evaluation.fairness_protocol: Mostly adequate: you specify (i) same online interaction budgets, (ii) same generator as parent without CGLC/ATCG, (iii) CGLC with fixed α=1, plus step-wise MBRL baselines, and multi-seed reporting. Missing/underspecified: exact data-collection policy parity (how trajectories are executed/replanned), equal wall-clock or equal model-update counts (important since Gramian/SVD cost varies), and hyperparameter budget fairness (λ_ctrl/λ_val/ATCG tuning should be matched across ablations/baselines). Also add a 'matched compute' condition where baselines get extra updates to offset Gramian overhead.
evaluation.feedback: This operator is a classic 'add a gate' incremental tweak (mechanism-commit-innovation on top of CGLC) and does not add a new scientific mechanism beyond confidence-weighting; ICML bar risk: novelty is low unless you can (a) justify sv_gap→calibrated α as a principled uncertainty estimator for controllability regularization, and (b) demonstrate new failure science (when/why Gramian misestimation hurts, and how gating changes that). To strengthen: (1) define sv_gap precisely and validate it as a predictor of Gramian estimation error (correlate sv_gap with held-out controllability/MCE estimation error or with downstream return deltas); (2) compare against simpler alternatives (hard threshold, EMA smoothing of λ_ctrl, or Bayesian/ensemble Gramian confidence) to show the calibration head is n...
evaluation.defect_fix_summary: Addresses defects ['Gramian misestimation', 'sensitivity to Gramian-confidence thresholding', partially 'over-conservatism'] by replacing a brittle fixed-strength controllability prior with a smooth, learned confidence gate that downweights λ_ctrl and attention bias when Gramian confidence is low, reducing harmful misconditioning from noisy low-rank Gramian estimates.

iterations.rationale: Add an Adaptive-Temperature Confidence Gate (ATCG) that modulates (a) the control-energy regularizer weight λ_ctrl and (b) the CGLC attention-bias scale as a smooth function of the Gramian-confidence metric. ATCG outputs a scalar α ∈ [0,1] computed from the Gramian-confidence (sv_gap) via a small monotonic neural calibration curve (2-layer MLP with sigmoid), producing effective λ_ctrl_eff = α · λ_ctrl_base and attention_bias_eff = α · attention_bias_base. When confidence is very low α→0 (falls back to identity-proxy behavior and disables strong control regularization); when confidence is high...

iterations.rationale: Add an Adaptive-Temperature Confidence Gate (ATCG) that modulates (a) the control-energy regularizer weight λ_ctrl and (b) the CGLC attention-bias scale as a smooth function of the Gramian-confidence metric. ATCG outputs a scalar α ∈ [0,1] computed from the Gramian-confidence (sv_gap) via a small monotonic neural calibration curve (2-layer MLP with sigmoid), producing effective λ_ctrl_eff = α · λ_ctrl_base and attention_bias_eff = α · attention_bias_base. When confidence is very low α→0 (falls back to identity-proxy behavior and disables strong control regularization); when confidence is high...

iterations.rationale: Add an Adaptive-Temperature Confidence Gate (ATCG) that modulates (a) the control-energy regularizer weight λ_ctrl and (b) the CGLC attention-bias scale as a smooth function of the Gramian-confidence metric. ATCG outputs a scalar α ∈ [0,1] computed from the Gramian-confidence (sv_gap) via a small monotonic neural calibration curve (2-layer MLP with sigmoid), producing effective λ_ctrl_eff = α · λ_ctrl_base and attention_bias_eff = α · attention_bias_base. When confidence is very low α→0 (falls back to identity-proxy behavior and disables strong control regularization); when confidence is high...

When confidence is very low α→0 (falls back to identity-proxy behavior and disables strong control regularization); when confidence is high...

Non-stationary online training causes the α-calibration to drift, yielding oscillatory λ_ctrl_eff that destabilizes flow-matching training
Adversarial/task-specific dynamics where high return requires temporarily high control energy; MCE prior + gating biases away from necessary maneuvers (over-co...
Reduced Gramian update frequency (compute-limited setting) makes α stale, leading to mis-gating and performance regressions under distribution shift
evaluation.fairness_protocol: Mostly adequate: you specify (i) same online interaction budgets, (ii) same generator as parent without CGLC/ATCG, (iii) CGLC with fixed α=1, plus step-wise MBRL baselines, and multi-seed reporting. Missing/underspecified: exact data-collection policy parity (how trajectories are executed/replanned), equal wall-clock or equal model-update counts (important since Gramian/SVD cost varies), and hyperparameter budget fairness (λ_ctrl/λ_val/ATCG tuning should be matched across ablations/baselines). Also add a 'matched compute' condition where baselines get extra updates to offset Gramian overhead.
evaluation.feedback: This operator is a classic 'add a gate' incremental tweak (mechanism-commit-innovation on top of CGLC) and does not add a new scientific mechanism beyond confidence-weighting; ICML bar risk: novelty is low unless you can (a) justify sv_gap→calibrated α as a principled uncertainty estimator for controllability regularization, and (b) demonstrate new failure science (when/why Gramian misestimation hurts, and how gating changes that). To strengthen: (1) define sv_gap precisely and validate it as a predictor of Gramian estimation error (correlate sv_gap with held-out controllability/MCE estimation error or with downstream return deltas); (2) compare against simpler alternatives (hard threshold, EMA smoothing of λ_ctrl, or Bayesian/ensemble Gramian confidence) to show the calibration head is n...
evaluation.defect_fix_summary: Addresses defects ['Gramian misestimation', 'sensitivity to Gramian-confidence thresholding', partially 'over-conservatism'] by replacing a brittle fixed-strength controllability prior with a smooth, learned confidence gate that downweights λ_ctrl and attention bias when Gramian confidence is low, reducing harmful misconditioning from noisy low-rank Gramian estimates.

evaluation.fairness_protocol: Mostly adequate: you specify (i) same online interaction budgets, (ii) same generator as parent without CGLC/ATCG, (iii) CGLC with fixed α=1, plus step-wise MBRL baselines, and multi-seed reporting. evaluation.feedback: This operator is a classic 'add a gate' incremental tweak (mechanism-commit-innovation on top of CGLC) and does not add a new scientific mechanism beyond confidence-weighting; ICML bar risk: novelty is low unless you can (a) justify sv_gap→calibrated α as a principled uncertainty estimator for controllability regularization, and (b) demonstrate new failure science (when/why Gramian misestimation hurts, and how gating changes that). evaluation.defect_fix_summary: Addresses defects ['Gramian misestimation', 'sensitivity to Gramian-confidence thresholding', partially 'over-conservatism'] by replacing a brittle fixed-strength controllability prior with a smooth, learned confidence gate that downweights λ_ctrl and attention bias when Gramian confidence is low, reducing harmful misconditioning from noisy low-rank Gramian estimates.

iterations.rationale: Add an Adaptive-Temperature Confidence Gate (ATCG) that modulates (a) the control-energy regularizer weight λ_ctrl and (b) the CGLC attention-bias scale as a smooth function of the Gramian-confidence metric. ATCG outputs a scalar α ∈ [0,1] computed from the Gramian-confidence (sv_gap) via a small monotonic neural calibration curve (2-layer MLP with sigmoid), producing effective λ_ctrl_eff = α · λ_ctrl_base and attention_bias_eff = α · attention_bias_base. When confidence is very low α→0 (falls back to identity-proxy behavior and disables strong control regularization); when confidence is high...

evaluation.fairness_protocol: Mostly adequate: you specify (i) same online interaction budgets, (ii) same generator as parent without CGLC/ATCG, (iii) CGLC with fixed α=1, plus step-wise MBRL baselines, and multi-seed reporting. evaluation.feedback: This operator is a classic 'add a gate' incremental tweak (mechanism-commit-innovation on top of CGLC) and does not add a new scientific mechanism beyond confidence-weighting; ICML bar risk: novelty is low unless you can (a) justify sv_gap→calibrated α as a principled uncertainty estimator for controllability regularization, and (b) demonstrate new failure science (when/why Gramian misestimation hurts, and how gating changes that). evaluation.defect_fix_summary: Addresses defects ['Gramian misestimation', 'sensitivity to Gramian-confidence thresholding', partially 'over-conservatism'] by replacing a brittle fixed-strength controllability prior with a smooth, learned confidence gate that downweights λ_ctrl and attention bias when Gramian confidence is low, reducing harmful misconditioning from noisy low-rank Gramian estimates.

iterations.rationale: Add an Adaptive-Temperature Confidence Gate (ATCG) that modulates (a) the control-energy regularizer weight λ_ctrl and (b) the CGLC attention-bias scale as a smooth function of the Gramian-confidence metric. ATCG outputs a scalar α ∈ [0,1] computed from the Gramian-confidence (sv_gap) via a small monotonic neural calibration curve (2-layer MLP with sigmoid), producing effective λ_ctrl_eff = α · λ_ctrl_base and attention_bias_eff = α · attention_bias_base. When confidence is very low α→0 (falls back to identity-proxy behavior and disables strong control regularization); when confidence is high...

evaluation.fairness_protocol: Mostly adequate: you specify (i) same online interaction budgets, (ii) same generator as parent without CGLC/ATCG, (iii) CGLC with fixed α=1, plus step-wise MBRL baselines, and multi-seed reporting. evaluation.feedback: This operator is a classic 'add a gate' incremental tweak (mechanism-commit-innovation on top of CGLC) and does not add a new scientific mechanism beyond confidence-weighting; ICML bar risk: novelty is low unless you can (a) justify sv_gap→calibrated α as a principled uncertainty estimator for controllability regularization, and (b) demonstrate new failure science (when/why Gramian misestimation hurts, and how gating changes that). evaluation.defect_fix_summary: Addresses defects ['Gramian misestimation', 'sensitivity to Gramian-confidence thresholding', partially 'over-conservatism'] by replacing a brittle fixed-strength controllability prior with a smooth, learned confidence gate that downweights λ_ctrl and attention bias when Gramian confidence is low, reducing harmful misconditioning from noisy low-rank Gramian estimates.

iterations.rationale: Add an Adaptive-Temperature Confidence Gate (ATCG) that modulates (a) the control-energy regularizer weight λ_ctrl and (b) the CGLC attention-bias scale as a smooth function of the Gramian-confidence metric. ATCG outputs a scalar α ∈ [0,1] computed from the Gramian-confidence (sv_gap) via a small monotonic neural calibration curve (2-layer MLP with sigmoid), producing effective λ_ctrl_eff = α · λ_ctrl_base and attention_bias_eff = α · attention_bias_base. When confidence is very low α→0 (falls back to identity-proxy behavior and disables strong control regularization); when confidence is high...

evaluation.fairness_protocol: Mostly adequate: you specify (i) same online interaction budgets, (ii) same generator as parent without CGLC/ATCG, (iii) CGLC with fixed α=1, plus step-wise MBRL baselines, and multi-seed reporting. evaluation.feedback: This operator is a classic 'add a gate' incremental tweak (mechanism-commit-innovation on top of CGLC) and does not add a new scientific mechanism beyond confidence-weighting; ICML bar risk: novelty is low unless you can (a) justify sv_gap→calibrated α as a principled uncertainty estimator for controllability regularization, and (b) demonstrate new failure science (when/why Gramian misestimation hurts, and how gating changes that). evaluation.defect_fix_summary: Addresses defects ['Gramian misestimation', 'sensitivity to Gramian-confidence thresholding', partially 'over-conservatism'] by replacing a brittle fixed-strength controllability prior with a smooth, learned confidence gate that downweights λ_ctrl and attention bias when Gramian confidence is low, reducing harmful misconditioning from noisy low-rank Gramian estimates.

iterations.rationale: Add an Adaptive-Temperature Confidence Gate (ATCG) that modulates (a) the control-energy regularizer weight λ_ctrl and (b) the CGLC attention-bias scale as a smooth function of the Gramian-confidence metric. ATCG outputs a scalar α ∈ [0,1] computed from the Gramian-confidence (sv_gap) via a small monotonic neural calibration curve (2-layer MLP with sigmoid), producing effective λ_ctrl_eff = α · λ_ctrl_base and attention_bias_eff = α · attention_bias_base. When confidence is very low α→0 (falls back to identity-proxy behavior and disables strong control regularization); when confidence is high...

evaluation.fairness_protocol: Mostly adequate: you specify (i) same online interaction budgets, (ii) same generator as parent without CGLC/ATCG, (iii) CGLC with fixed α=1, plus step-wise MBRL baselines, and multi-seed reporting. evaluation.feedback: This operator is a classic 'add a gate' incremental tweak (mechanism-commit-innovation on top of CGLC) and does not add a new scientific mechanism beyond confidence-weighting; ICML bar risk: novelty is low unless you can (a) justify sv_gap→calibrated α as a principled uncertainty estimator for controllability regularization, and (b) demonstrate new failure science (when/why Gramian misestimation hurts, and how gating changes that). evaluation.defect_fix_summary: Addresses defects ['Gramian misestimation', 'sensitivity to Gramian-confidence thresholding', partially 'over-conservatism'] by replacing a brittle fixed-strength controllability prior with a smooth, learned confidence gate that downweights λ_ctrl and attention bias when Gramian confidence is low, reducing harmful misconditioning from noisy low-rank Gramian estimates.

iterations.rationale: Add an Adaptive-Temperature Confidence Gate (ATCG) that modulates (a) the control-energy regularizer weight λ_ctrl and (b) the CGLC attention-bias scale as a smooth function of the Gramian-confidence metric. ATCG outputs a scalar α ∈ [0,1] computed from the Gramian-confidence (sv_gap) via a small monotonic neural calibration curve (2-layer MLP with sigmoid), producing effective λ_ctrl_eff = α · λ_ctrl_base and attention_bias_eff = α · attention_bias_base. When confidence is very low α→0 (falls back to identity-proxy behavior and disables strong control regularization); when confidence is high...

evaluation.fairness_protocol: Mostly adequate: you specify (i) same online interaction budgets, (ii) same generator as parent without CGLC/ATCG, (iii) CGLC with fixed α=1, plus step-wise MBRL baselines, and multi-seed reporting. evaluation.feedback: This operator is a classic 'add a gate' incremental tweak (mechanism-commit-innovation on top of CGLC) and does not add a new scientific mechanism beyond confidence-weighting; ICML bar risk: novelty is low unless you can (a) justify sv_gap→calibrated α as a principled uncertainty estimator for controllability regularization, and (b) demonstrate new failure science (when/why Gramian misestimation hurts, and how gating changes that). evaluation.defect_fix_summary: Addresses defects ['Gramian misestimation', 'sensitivity to Gramian-confidence thresholding', partially 'over-conservatism'] by replacing a brittle fixed-strength controllability prior with a smooth, learned confidence gate that downweights λ_ctrl and attention bias when Gramian confidence is low, reducing harmful misconditioning from noisy low-rank Gramian estimates.

iterations.rationale: Add an Adaptive-Temperature Confidence Gate (ATCG) that modulates (a) the control-energy regularizer weight λ_ctrl and (b) the CGLC attention-bias scale as a smooth function of the Gramian-confidence metric. ATCG outputs a scalar α ∈ [0,1] computed from the Gramian-confidence (sv_gap) via a small monotonic neural calibration curve (2-layer MLP with sigmoid), producing effective λ_ctrl_eff = α · λ_ctrl_base and attention_bias_eff = α · attention_bias_base. When confidence is very low α→0 (falls back to identity-proxy behavior and disables strong control regularization); when confidence is high...

evaluation.fairness_protocol: Mostly adequate: you specify (i) same online interaction budgets, (ii) same generator as parent without CGLC/ATCG, (iii) CGLC with fixed α=1, plus step-wise MBRL baselines, and multi-seed reporting. evaluation.feedback: This operator is a classic 'add a gate' incremental tweak (mechanism-commit-innovation on top of CGLC) and does not add a new scientific mechanism beyond confidence-weighting; ICML bar risk: novelty is low unless you can (a) justify sv_gap→calibrated α as a principled uncertainty estimator for controllability regularization, and (b) demonstrate new failure science (when/why Gramian misestimation hurts, and how gating changes that). evaluation.defect_fix_summary: Addresses defects ['Gramian misestimation', 'sensitivity to Gramian-confidence thresholding', partially 'over-conservatism'] by replacing a brittle fixed-strength controllability prior with a smooth, learned confidence gate that downweights λ_ctrl and attention bias when Gramian confidence is low, reducing harmful misconditioning from noisy low-rank Gramian estimates.

iterations.rationale: Add an Adaptive-Temperature Confidence Gate (ATCG) that modulates (a) the control-energy regularizer weight λ_ctrl and (b) the CGLC attention-bias scale as a smooth function of the Gramian-confidence metric. ATCG outputs a scalar α ∈ [0,1] computed from the Gramian-confidence (sv_gap) via a small monotonic neural calibration curve (2-layer MLP with sigmoid), producing effective λ_ctrl_eff = α · λ_ctrl_base and attention_bias_eff = α · attention_bias_base. When confidence is very low α→0 (falls back to identity-proxy behavior and disables strong control regularization); when confidence is high...

evaluation.fairness_protocol: Mostly adequate: you specify (i) same online interaction budgets, (ii) same generator as parent without CGLC/ATCG, (iii) CGLC with fixed α=1, plus step-wise MBRL baselines, and multi-seed reporting. evaluation.feedback: This operator is a classic 'add a gate' incremental tweak (mechanism-commit-innovation on top of CGLC) and does not add a new scientific mechanism beyond confidence-weighting; ICML bar risk: novelty is low unless you can (a) justify sv_gap→calibrated α as a principled uncertainty estimator for controllability regularization, and (b) demonstrate new failure science (when/why Gramian misestimation hurts, and how gating changes that). evaluation.defect_fix_summary: Addresses defects ['Gramian misestimation', 'sensitivity to Gramian-confidence thresholding', partially 'over-conservatism'] by replacing a brittle fixed-strength controllability prior with a smooth, learned confidence gate that downweights λ_ctrl and attention bias when Gramian confidence is low, reducing harmful misconditioning from noisy low-rank Gramian estimates.

iterations.rationale: Add an Adaptive-Temperature Confidence Gate (ATCG) that modulates (a) the control-energy regularizer weight λ_ctrl and (b) the CGLC attention-bias scale as a smooth function of the Gramian-confidence metric. ATCG outputs a scalar α ∈ [0,1] computed from the Gramian-confidence (sv_gap) via a small monotonic neural calibration curve (2-layer MLP with sigmoid), producing effective λ_ctrl_eff = α · λ_ctrl_base and attention_bias_eff = α · attention_bias_base. When confidence is very low α→0 (falls back to identity-proxy behavior and disables strong control regularization); when confidence is high...

evaluation.fairness_protocol: Mostly adequate: you specify (i) same online interaction budgets, (ii) same generator as parent without CGLC/ATCG, (iii) CGLC with fixed α=1, plus step-wise MBRL baselines, and multi-seed reporting. evaluation.feedback: This operator is a classic 'add a gate' incremental tweak (mechanism-commit-innovation on top of CGLC) and does not add a new scientific mechanism beyond confidence-weighting; ICML bar risk: novelty is low unless you can (a) justify sv_gap→calibrated α as a principled uncertainty estimator for controllability regularization, and (b) demonstrate new failure science (when/why Gramian misestimation hurts, and how gating changes that). evaluation.defect_fix_summary: Addresses defects ['Gramian misestimation', 'sensitivity to Gramian-confidence thresholding', partially 'over-conservatism'] by replacing a brittle fixed-strength controllability prior with a smooth, learned confidence gate that downweights λ_ctrl and attention bias when Gramian confidence is low, reducing harmful misconditioning from noisy low-rank Gramian estimates.

iterations.rationale: Add an Adaptive-Temperature Confidence Gate (ATCG) that modulates (a) the control-energy regularizer weight λ_ctrl and (b) the CGLC attention-bias scale as a smooth function of the Gramian-confidence metric. ATCG outputs a scalar α ∈ [0,1] computed from the Gramian-confidence (sv_gap) via a small monotonic neural calibration curve (2-layer MLP with sigmoid), producing effective λ_ctrl_eff = α · λ_ctrl_base and attention_bias_eff = α · attention_bias_base. When confidence is very low α→0 (falls back to identity-proxy behavior and disables strong control regularization); when confidence is high...

evaluation.fairness_protocol: Mostly adequate: you specify (i) same online interaction budgets, (ii) same generator as parent without CGLC/ATCG, (iii) CGLC with fixed α=1, plus step-wise MBRL baselines, and multi-seed reporting. evaluation.feedback: This operator is a classic 'add a gate' incremental tweak (mechanism-commit-innovation on top of CGLC) and does not add a new scientific mechanism beyond confidence-weighting; ICML bar risk: novelty is low unless you can (a) justify sv_gap→calibrated α as a principled uncertainty estimator for controllability regularization, and (b) demonstrate new failure science (when/why Gramian misestimation hurts, and how gating changes that). evaluation.defect_fix_summary: Addresses defects ['Gramian misestimation', 'sensitivity to Gramian-confidence thresholding', partially 'over-conservatism'] by replacing a brittle fixed-strength controllability prior with a smooth, learned confidence gate that downweights λ_ctrl and attention bias when Gramian confidence is low, reducing harmful misconditioning from noisy low-rank Gramian estimates.

iterations.rationale: Add an Adaptive-Temperature Confidence Gate (ATCG) that modulates (a) the control-energy regularizer weight λ_ctrl and (b) the CGLC attention-bias scale as a smooth function of the Gramian-confidence metric. ATCG outputs a scalar α ∈ [0,1] computed from the Gramian-confidence (sv_gap) via a small monotonic neural calibration curve (2-layer MLP with sigmoid), producing effective λ_ctrl_eff = α · λ_ctrl_base and attention_bias_eff = α · attention_bias_base. When confidence is very low α→0 (falls back to identity-proxy behavior and disables strong control regularization); when confidence is high...

evaluation.fairness_protocol: Mostly adequate: you specify (i) same online interaction budgets, (ii) same generator as parent without CGLC/ATCG, (iii) CGLC with fixed α=1, plus step-wise MBRL baselines, and multi-seed reporting. evaluation.feedback: This operator is a classic 'add a gate' incremental tweak (mechanism-commit-innovation on top of CGLC) and does not add a new scientific mechanism beyond confidence-weighting; ICML bar risk: novelty is low unless you can (a) justify sv_gap→calibrated α as a principled uncertainty estimator for controllability regularization, and (b) demonstrate new failure science (when/why Gramian misestimation hurts, and how gating changes that). evaluation.defect_fix_summary: Addresses defects ['Gramian misestimation', 'sensitivity to Gramian-confidence thresholding', partially 'over-conservatism'] by replacing a brittle fixed-strength controllability prior with a smooth, learned confidence gate that downweights λ_ctrl and attention bias when Gramian confidence is low, reducing harmful misconditioning from noisy low-rank Gramian estimates.

iterations.rationale: Add an Adaptive-Temperature Confidence Gate (ATCG) that modulates (a) the control-energy regularizer weight λ_ctrl and (b) the CGLC attention-bias scale as a smooth function of the Gramian-confidence metric. ATCG outputs a scalar α ∈ [0,1] computed from the Gramian-confidence (sv_gap) via a small monotonic neural calibration curve (2-layer MLP with sigmoid), producing effective λ_ctrl_eff = α · λ_ctrl_base and attention_bias_eff = α · attention_bias_base. When confidence is very low α→0 (falls back to identity-proxy behavior and disables strong control regularization); when confidence is high...

evaluation.fairness_protocol: Mostly adequate: you specify (i) same online interaction budgets, (ii) same generator as parent without CGLC/ATCG, (iii) CGLC with fixed α=1, plus step-wise MBRL baselines, and multi-seed reporting. evaluation.feedback: This operator is a classic 'add a gate' incremental tweak (mechanism-commit-innovation on top of CGLC) and does not add a new scientific mechanism beyond confidence-weighting; ICML bar risk: novelty is low unless you can (a) justify sv_gap→calibrated α as a principled uncertainty estimator for controllability regularization, and (b) demonstrate new failure science (when/why Gramian misestimation hurts, and how gating changes that). evaluation.defect_fix_summary: Addresses defects ['Gramian misestimation', 'sensitivity to Gramian-confidence thresholding', partially 'over-conservatism'] by replacing a brittle fixed-strength controllability prior with a smooth, learned confidence gate that downweights λ_ctrl and attention bias when Gramian confidence is low, reducing harmful misconditioning from noisy low-rank Gramian estimates.

iterations.rationale: Add an Adaptive-Temperature Confidence Gate (ATCG) that modulates (a) the control-energy regularizer weight λ_ctrl and (b) the CGLC attention-bias scale as a smooth function of the Gramian-confidence metric. ATCG outputs a scalar α ∈ [0,1] computed from the Gramian-confidence (sv_gap) via a small monotonic neural calibration curve (2-layer MLP with sigmoid), producing effective λ_ctrl_eff = α · λ_ctrl_base and attention_bias_eff = α · attention_bias_base. When confidence is very low α→0 (falls back to identity-proxy behavior and disables strong control regularization); when confidence is high...

evaluation.fairness_protocol: Mostly adequate: you specify (i) same online interaction budgets, (ii) same generator as parent without CGLC/ATCG, (iii) CGLC with fixed α=1, plus step-wise MBRL baselines, and multi-seed reporting. evaluation.feedback: This operator is a classic 'add a gate' incremental tweak (mechanism-commit-innovation on top of CGLC) and does not add a new scientific mechanism beyond confidence-weighting; ICML bar risk: novelty is low unless you can (a) justify sv_gap→calibrated α as a principled uncertainty estimator for controllability regularization, and (b) demonstrate new failure science (when/why Gramian misestimation hurts, and how gating changes that). evaluation.defect_fix_summary: Addresses defects ['Gramian misestimation', 'sensitivity to Gramian-confidence thresholding', partially 'over-conservatism'] by replacing a brittle fixed-strength controllability prior with a smooth, learned confidence gate that downweights λ_ctrl and attention bias when Gramian confidence is low, reducing harmful misconditioning from noisy low-rank Gramian estimates.

iterations.rationale: Add an Adaptive-Temperature Confidence Gate (ATCG) that modulates (a) the control-energy regularizer weight λ_ctrl and (b) the CGLC attention-bias scale as a smooth function of the Gramian-confidence metric. ATCG outputs a scalar α ∈ [0,1] computed from the Gramian-confidence (sv_gap) via a small monotonic neural calibration curve (2-layer MLP with sigmoid), producing effective λ_ctrl_eff = α · λ_ctrl_base and attention_bias_eff = α · attention_bias_base. When confidence is very low α→0 (falls back to identity-proxy behavior and disables strong control regularization); when confidence is high...

evaluation.fairness_protocol: Mostly adequate: you specify (i) same online interaction budgets, (ii) same generator as parent without CGLC/ATCG, (iii) CGLC with fixed α=1, plus step-wise MBRL baselines, and multi-seed reporting. evaluation.feedback: This operator is a classic 'add a gate' incremental tweak (mechanism-commit-innovation on top of CGLC) and does not add a new scientific mechanism beyond confidence-weighting; ICML bar risk: novelty is low unless you can (a) justify sv_gap→calibrated α as a principled uncertainty estimator for controllability regularization, and (b) demonstrate new failure science (when/why Gramian misestimation hurts, and how gating changes that). evaluation.defect_fix_summary: Addresses defects ['Gramian misestimation', 'sensitivity to Gramian-confidence thresholding', partially 'over-conservatism'] by replacing a brittle fixed-strength controllability prior with a smooth, learned confidence gate that downweights λ_ctrl and attention bias when Gramian confidence is low, reducing harmful misconditioning from noisy low-rank Gramian estimates.

iterations.rationale: Add an Adaptive-Temperature Confidence Gate (ATCG) that modulates (a) the control-energy regularizer weight λ_ctrl and (b) the CGLC attention-bias scale as a smooth function of the Gramian-confidence metric. ATCG outputs a scalar α ∈ [0,1] computed from the Gramian-confidence (sv_gap) via a small monotonic neural calibration curve (2-layer MLP with sigmoid), producing effective λ_ctrl_eff = α · λ_ctrl_base and attention_bias_eff = α · attention_bias_base. When confidence is very low α→0 (falls back to identity-proxy behavior and disables strong control regularization); when confidence is high...

evaluation.fairness_protocol: Mostly adequate: you specify (i) same online interaction budgets, (ii) same generator as parent without CGLC/ATCG, (iii) CGLC with fixed α=1, plus step-wise MBRL baselines, and multi-seed reporting. evaluation.feedback: This operator is a classic 'add a gate' incremental tweak (mechanism-commit-innovation on top of CGLC) and does not add a new scientific mechanism beyond confidence-weighting; ICML bar risk: novelty is low unless you can (a) justify sv_gap→calibrated α as a principled uncertainty estimator for controllability regularization, and (b) demonstrate new failure science (when/why Gramian misestimation hurts, and how gating changes that). evaluation.defect_fix_summary: Addresses defects ['Gramian misestimation', 'sensitivity to Gramian-confidence thresholding', partially 'over-conservatism'] by replacing a brittle fixed-strength controllability prior with a smooth, learned confidence gate that downweights λ_ctrl and attention bias when Gramian confidence is low, reducing harmful misconditioning from noisy low-rank Gramian estimates.

iterations.rationale: Add an Adaptive-Temperature Confidence Gate (ATCG) that modulates (a) the control-energy regularizer weight λ_ctrl and (b) the CGLC attention-bias scale as a smooth function of the Gramian-confidence metric. ATCG outputs a scalar α ∈ [0,1] computed from the Gramian-confidence (sv_gap) via a small monotonic neural calibration curve (2-layer MLP with sigmoid), producing effective λ_ctrl_eff = α · λ_ctrl_base and attention_bias_eff = α · attention_bias_base. When confidence is very low α→0 (falls back to identity-proxy behavior and disables strong control regularization); when confidence is high...

evaluation.fairness_protocol: Mostly adequate: you specify (i) same online interaction budgets, (ii) same generator as parent without CGLC/ATCG, (iii) CGLC with fixed α=1, plus step-wise MBRL baselines, and multi-seed reporting. evaluation.feedback: This operator is a classic 'add a gate' incremental tweak (mechanism-commit-innovation on top of CGLC) and does not add a new scientific mechanism beyond confidence-weighting; ICML bar risk: novelty is low unless you can (a) justify sv_gap→calibrated α as a principled uncertainty estimator for controllability regularization, and (b) demonstrate new failure science (when/why Gramian misestimation hurts, and how gating changes that). evaluation.defect_fix_summary: Addresses defects ['Gramian misestimation', 'sensitivity to Gramian-confidence thresholding', partially 'over-conservatism'] by replacing a brittle fixed-strength controllability prior with a smooth, learned confidence gate that downweights λ_ctrl and attention bias when Gramian confidence is low, reducing harmful misconditioning from noisy low-rank Gramian estimates.

iterations.rationale: Add an Adaptive-Temperature Confidence Gate (ATCG) that modulates (a) the control-energy regularizer weight λ_ctrl and (b) the CGLC attention-bias scale as a smooth function of the Gramian-confidence metric. ATCG outputs a scalar α ∈ [0,1] computed from the Gramian-confidence (sv_gap) via a small monotonic neural calibration curve (2-layer MLP with sigmoid), producing effective λ_ctrl_eff = α · λ_ctrl_base and attention_bias_eff = α · attention_bias_base. When confidence is very low α→0 (falls back to identity-proxy behavior and disables strong control regularization); when confidence is high...

evaluation.fairness_protocol: Mostly adequate: you specify (i) same online interaction budgets, (ii) same generator as parent without CGLC/ATCG, (iii) CGLC with fixed α=1, plus step-wise MBRL baselines, and multi-seed reporting. evaluation.feedback: This operator is a classic 'add a gate' incremental tweak (mechanism-commit-innovation on top of CGLC) and does not add a new scientific mechanism beyond confidence-weighting; ICML bar risk: novelty is low unless you can (a) justify sv_gap→calibrated α as a principled uncertainty estimator for controllability regularization, and (b) demonstrate new failure science (when/why Gramian misestimation hurts, and how gating changes that). evaluation.defect_fix_summary: Addresses defects ['Gramian misestimation', 'sensitivity to Gramian-confidence thresholding', partially 'over-conservatism'] by replacing a brittle fixed-strength controllability prior with a smooth, learned confidence gate that downweights λ_ctrl and attention bias when Gramian confidence is low, reducing harmful misconditioning from noisy low-rank Gramian estimates.

iterations.rationale: Add an Adaptive-Temperature Confidence Gate (ATCG) that modulates (a) the control-energy regularizer weight λ_ctrl and (b) the CGLC attention-bias scale as a smooth function of the Gramian-confidence metric. ATCG outputs a scalar α ∈ [0,1] computed from the Gramian-confidence (sv_gap) via a small monotonic neural calibration curve (2-layer MLP with sigmoid), producing effective λ_ctrl_eff = α · λ_ctrl_base and attention_bias_eff = α · attention_bias_base. When confidence is very low α→0 (falls back to identity-proxy behavior and disables strong control regularization); when confidence is high...

evaluation.fairness_protocol: Mostly adequate: you specify (i) same online interaction budgets, (ii) same generator as parent without CGLC/ATCG, (iii) CGLC with fixed α=1, plus step-wise MBRL baselines, and multi-seed reporting. evaluation.feedback: This operator is a classic 'add a gate' incremental tweak (mechanism-commit-innovation on top of CGLC) and does not add a new scientific mechanism beyond confidence-weighting; ICML bar risk: novelty is low unless you can (a) justify sv_gap→calibrated α as a principled uncertainty estimator for controllability regularization, and (b) demonstrate new failure science (when/why Gramian misestimation hurts, and how gating changes that). evaluation.defect_fix_summary: Addresses defects ['Gramian misestimation', 'sensitivity to Gramian-confidence thresholding', partially 'over-conservatism'] by replacing a brittle fixed-strength controllability prior with a smooth, learned confidence gate that downweights λ_ctrl and attention bias when Gramian confidence is low, reducing harmful misconditioning from noisy low-rank Gramian estimates.

iterations.rationale: Add an Adaptive-Temperature Confidence Gate (ATCG) that modulates (a) the control-energy regularizer weight λ_ctrl and (b) the CGLC attention-bias scale as a smooth function of the Gramian-confidence metric. ATCG outputs a scalar α ∈ [0,1] computed from the Gramian-confidence (sv_gap) via a small monotonic neural calibration curve (2-layer MLP with sigmoid), producing effective λ_ctrl_eff = α · λ_ctrl_base and attention_bias_eff = α · attention_bias_base. When confidence is very low α→0 (falls back to identity-proxy behavior and disables strong control regularization); when confidence is high...

evaluation.fairness_protocol: Mostly adequate: you specify (i) same online interaction budgets, (ii) same generator as parent without CGLC/ATCG, (iii) CGLC with fixed α=1, plus step-wise MBRL baselines, and multi-seed reporting. evaluation.feedback: This operator is a classic 'add a gate' incremental tweak (mechanism-commit-innovation on top of CGLC) and does not add a new scientific mechanism beyond confidence-weighting; ICML bar risk: novelty is low unless you can (a) justify sv_gap→calibrated α as a principled uncertainty estimator for controllability regularization, and (b) demonstrate new failure science (when/why Gramian misestimation hurts, and how gating changes that). evaluation.defect_fix_summary: Addresses defects ['Gramian misestimation', 'sensitivity to Gramian-confidence thresholding', partially 'over-conservatism'] by replacing a brittle fixed-strength controllability prior with a smooth, learned confidence gate that downweights λ_ctrl and attention bias when Gramian confidence is low, reducing harmful misconditioning from noisy low-rank Gramian estimates.

iterations.rationale: Add an Adaptive-Temperature Confidence Gate (ATCG) that modulates (a) the control-energy regularizer weight λ_ctrl and (b) the CGLC attention-bias scale as a smooth function of the Gramian-confidence metric. ATCG outputs a scalar α ∈ [0,1] computed from the Gramian-confidence (sv_gap) via a small monotonic neural calibration curve (2-layer MLP with sigmoid), producing effective λ_ctrl_eff = α · λ_ctrl_base and attention_bias_eff = α · attention_bias_base. When confidence is very low α→0 (falls back to identity-proxy behavior and disables strong control regularization); when confidence is high...

evaluation.fairness_protocol: Mostly adequate: you specify (i) same online interaction budgets, (ii) same generator as parent without CGLC/ATCG, (iii) CGLC with fixed α=1, plus step-wise MBRL baselines, and multi-seed reporting. evaluation.feedback: This operator is a classic 'add a gate' incremental tweak (mechanism-commit-innovation on top of CGLC) and does not add a new scientific mechanism beyond confidence-weighting; ICML bar risk: novelty is low unless you can (a) justify sv_gap→calibrated α as a principled uncertainty estimator for controllability regularization, and (b) demonstrate new failure science (when/why Gramian misestimation hurts, and how gating changes that). evaluation.defect_fix_summary: Addresses defects ['Gramian misestimation', 'sensitivity to Gramian-confidence thresholding', partially 'over-conservatism'] by replacing a brittle fixed-strength controllability prior with a smooth, learned confidence gate that downweights λ_ctrl and attention bias when Gramian confidence is low, reducing harmful misconditioning from noisy low-rank Gramian estimates.

iterations.rationale: Add an Adaptive-Temperature Confidence Gate (ATCG) that modulates (a) the control-energy regularizer weight λ_ctrl and (b) the CGLC attention-bias scale as a smooth function of the Gramian-confidence metric. ATCG outputs a scalar α ∈ [0,1] computed from the Gramian-confidence (sv_gap) via a small monotonic neural calibration curve (2-layer MLP with sigmoid), producing effective λ_ctrl_eff = α · λ_ctrl_base and attention_bias_eff = α · attention_bias_base. When confidence is very low α→0 (falls back to identity-proxy behavior and disables strong control regularization); when confidence is high...

evaluation.fairness_protocol: Mostly adequate: you specify (i) same online interaction budgets, (ii) same generator as parent without CGLC/ATCG, (iii) CGLC with fixed α=1, plus step-wise MBRL baselines, and multi-seed reporting. evaluation.feedback: This operator is a classic 'add a gate' incremental tweak (mechanism-commit-innovation on top of CGLC) and does not add a new scientific mechanism beyond confidence-weighting; ICML bar risk: novelty is low unless you can (a) justify sv_gap→calibrated α as a principled uncertainty estimator for controllability regularization, and (b) demonstrate new failure science (when/why Gramian misestimation hurts, and how gating changes that). evaluation.defect_fix_summary: Addresses defects ['Gramian misestimation', 'sensitivity to Gramian-confidence thresholding', partially 'over-conservatism'] by replacing a brittle fixed-strength controllability prior with a smooth, learned confidence gate that downweights λ_ctrl and attention bias when Gramian confidence is low, reducing harmful misconditioning from noisy low-rank Gramian estimates.

iterations.rationale: Add an Adaptive-Temperature Confidence Gate (ATCG) that modulates (a) the control-energy regularizer weight λ_ctrl and (b) the CGLC attention-bias scale as a smooth function of the Gramian-confidence metric. ATCG outputs a scalar α ∈ [0,1] computed from the Gramian-confidence (sv_gap) via a small monotonic neural calibration curve (2-layer MLP with sigmoid), producing effective λ_ctrl_eff = α · λ_ctrl_base and attention_bias_eff = α · attention_bias_base. When confidence is very low α→0 (falls back to identity-proxy behavior and disables strong control regularization); when confidence is high...

evaluation.fairness_protocol: Mostly adequate: you specify (i) same online interaction budgets, (ii) same generator as parent without CGLC/ATCG, (iii) CGLC with fixed α=1, plus step-wise MBRL baselines, and multi-seed reporting. evaluation.feedback: This operator is a classic 'add a gate' incremental tweak (mechanism-commit-innovation on top of CGLC) and does not add a new scientific mechanism beyond confidence-weighting; ICML bar risk: novelty is low unless you can (a) justify sv_gap→calibrated α as a principled uncertainty estimator for controllability regularization, and (b) demonstrate new failure science (when/why Gramian misestimation hurts, and how gating changes that). evaluation.defect_fix_summary: Addresses defects ['Gramian misestimation', 'sensitivity to Gramian-confidence thresholding', partially 'over-conservatism'] by replacing a brittle fixed-strength controllability prior with a smooth, learned confidence gate that downweights λ_ctrl and attention bias when Gramian confidence is low, reducing harmful misconditioning from noisy low-rank Gramian estimates.

iterations.rationale: Add an Adaptive-Temperature Confidence Gate (ATCG) that modulates (a) the control-energy regularizer weight λ_ctrl and (b) the CGLC attention-bias scale as a smooth function of the Gramian-confidence metric. ATCG outputs a scalar α ∈ [0,1] computed from the Gramian-confidence (sv_gap) via a small monotonic neural calibration curve (2-layer MLP with sigmoid), producing effective λ_ctrl_eff = α · λ_ctrl_base and attention_bias_eff = α · attention_bias_base. When confidence is very low α→0 (falls back to identity-proxy behavior and disables strong control regularization); when confidence is high...

evaluation.fairness_protocol: Mostly adequate: you specify (i) same online interaction budgets, (ii) same generator as parent without CGLC/ATCG, (iii) CGLC with fixed α=1, plus step-wise MBRL baselines, and multi-seed reporting. evaluation.feedback: This operator is a classic 'add a gate' incremental tweak (mechanism-commit-innovation on top of CGLC) and does not add a new scientific mechanism beyond confidence-weighting; ICML bar risk: novelty is low unless you can (a) justify sv_gap→calibrated α as a principled uncertainty estimator for controllability regularization, and (b) demonstrate new failure science (when/why Gramian misestimation hurts, and how gating changes that). evaluation.defect_fix_summary: Addresses defects ['Gramian misestimation', 'sensitivity to Gramian-confidence thresholding', partially 'over-conservatism'] by replacing a brittle fixed-strength controllability prior with a smooth, learned confidence gate that downweights λ_ctrl and attention bias when Gramian confidence is low, reducing harmful misconditioning from noisy low-rank Gramian estimates.

iterations.rationale: Add an Adaptive-Temperature Confidence Gate (ATCG) that modulates (a) the control-energy regularizer weight λ_ctrl and (b) the CGLC attention-bias scale as a smooth function of the Gramian-confidence metric. ATCG outputs a scalar α ∈ [0,1] computed from the Gramian-confidence (sv_gap) via a small monotonic neural calibration curve (2-layer MLP with sigmoid), producing effective λ_ctrl_eff = α · λ_ctrl_base and attention_bias_eff = α · attention_bias_base. When confidence is very low α→0 (falls back to identity-proxy behavior and disables strong control regularization); when confidence is high...

evaluation.fairness_protocol: Mostly adequate: you specify (i) same online interaction budgets, (ii) same generator as parent without CGLC/ATCG, (iii) CGLC with fixed α=1, plus step-wise MBRL baselines, and multi-seed reporting. evaluation.feedback: This operator is a classic 'add a gate' incremental tweak (mechanism-commit-innovation on top of CGLC) and does not add a new scientific mechanism beyond confidence-weighting; ICML bar risk: novelty is low unless you can (a) justify sv_gap→calibrated α as a principled uncertainty estimator for controllability regularization, and (b) demonstrate new failure science (when/why Gramian misestimation hurts, and how gating changes that). evaluation.defect_fix_summary: Addresses defects ['Gramian misestimation', 'sensitivity to Gramian-confidence thresholding', partially 'over-conservatism'] by replacing a brittle fixed-strength controllability prior with a smooth, learned confidence gate that downweights λ_ctrl and attention bias when Gramian confidence is low, reducing harmful misconditioning from noisy low-rank Gramian estimates.

iterations.rationale: Add an Adaptive-Temperature Confidence Gate (ATCG) that modulates (a) the control-energy regularizer weight λ_ctrl and (b) the CGLC attention-bias scale as a smooth function of the Gramian-confidence metric. ATCG outputs a scalar α ∈ [0,1] computed from the Gramian-confidence (sv_gap) via a small monotonic neural calibration curve (2-layer MLP with sigmoid), producing effective λ_ctrl_eff = α · λ_ctrl_base and attention_bias_eff = α · attention_bias_base. When confidence is very low α→0 (falls back to identity-proxy behavior and disables strong control regularization); when confidence is high...

evaluation.fairness_protocol: Mostly adequate: you specify (i) same online interaction budgets, (ii) same generator as parent without CGLC/ATCG, (iii) CGLC with fixed α=1, plus step-wise MBRL baselines, and multi-seed reporting. evaluation.feedback: This operator is a classic 'add a gate' incremental tweak (mechanism-commit-innovation on top of CGLC) and does not add a new scientific mechanism beyond confidence-weighting; ICML bar risk: novelty is low unless you can (a) justify sv_gap→calibrated α as a principled uncertainty estimator for controllability regularization, and (b) demonstrate new failure science (when/why Gramian misestimation hurts, and how gating changes that). evaluation.defect_fix_summary: Addresses defects ['Gramian misestimation', 'sensitivity to Gramian-confidence thresholding', partially 'over-conservatism'] by replacing a brittle fixed-strength controllability prior with a smooth, learned confidence gate that downweights λ_ctrl and attention bias when Gramian confidence is low, reducing harmful misconditioning from noisy low-rank Gramian estimates.

iterations.rationale: Add an Adaptive-Temperature Confidence Gate (ATCG) that modulates (a) the control-energy regularizer weight λ_ctrl and (b) the CGLC attention-bias scale as a smooth function of the Gramian-confidence metric. ATCG outputs a scalar α ∈ [0,1] computed from the Gramian-confidence (sv_gap) via a small monotonic neural calibration curve (2-layer MLP with sigmoid), producing effective λ_ctrl_eff = α · λ_ctrl_base and attention_bias_eff = α · attention_bias_base. When confidence is very low α→0 (falls back to identity-proxy behavior and disables strong control regularization); when confidence is high...

evaluation.fairness_protocol: Mostly adequate: you specify (i) same online interaction budgets, (ii) same generator as parent without CGLC/ATCG, (iii) CGLC with fixed α=1, plus step-wise MBRL baselines, and multi-seed reporting. evaluation.feedback: This operator is a classic 'add a gate' incremental tweak (mechanism-commit-innovation on top of CGLC) and does not add a new scientific mechanism beyond confidence-weighting; ICML bar risk: novelty is low unless you can (a) justify sv_gap→calibrated α as a principled uncertainty estimator for controllability regularization, and (b) demonstrate new failure science (when/why Gramian misestimation hurts, and how gating changes that). evaluation.defect_fix_summary: Addresses defects ['Gramian misestimation', 'sensitivity to Gramian-confidence thresholding', partially 'over-conservatism'] by replacing a brittle fixed-strength controllability prior with a smooth, learned confidence gate that downweights λ_ctrl and attention bias when Gramian confidence is low, reducing harmful misconditioning from noisy low-rank Gramian estimates.

iterations.rationale: Add an Adaptive-Temperature Confidence Gate (ATCG) that modulates (a) the control-energy regularizer weight λ_ctrl and (b) the CGLC attention-bias scale as a smooth function of the Gramian-confidence metric. ATCG outputs a scalar α ∈ [0,1] computed from the Gramian-confidence (sv_gap) via a small monotonic neural calibration curve (2-layer MLP with sigmoid), producing effective λ_ctrl_eff = α · λ_ctrl_base and attention_bias_eff = α · attention_bias_base. When confidence is very low α→0 (falls back to identity-proxy behavior and disables strong control regularization); when confidence is high...

evaluation.fairness_protocol: Mostly adequate: you specify (i) same online interaction budgets, (ii) same generator as parent without CGLC/ATCG, (iii) CGLC with fixed α=1, plus step-wise MBRL baselines, and multi-seed reporting. evaluation.feedback: This operator is a classic 'add a gate' incremental tweak (mechanism-commit-innovation on top of CGLC) and does not add a new scientific mechanism beyond confidence-weighting; ICML bar risk: novelty is low unless you can (a) justify sv_gap→calibrated α as a principled uncertainty estimator for controllability regularization, and (b) demonstrate new failure science (when/why Gramian misestimation hurts, and how gating changes that). evaluation.defect_fix_summary: Addresses defects ['Gramian misestimation', 'sensitivity to Gramian-confidence thresholding', partially 'over-conservatism'] by replacing a brittle fixed-strength controllability prior with a smooth, learned confidence gate that downweights λ_ctrl and attention bias when Gramian confidence is low, reducing harmful misconditioning from noisy low-rank Gramian estimates.

iterations.rationale: Add an Adaptive-Temperature Confidence Gate (ATCG) that modulates (a) the control-energy regularizer weight λ_ctrl and (b) the CGLC attention-bias scale as a smooth function of the Gramian-confidence metric. ATCG outputs a scalar α ∈ [0,1] computed from the Gramian-confidence (sv_gap) via a small monotonic neural calibration curve (2-layer MLP with sigmoid), producing effective λ_ctrl_eff = α · λ_ctrl_base and attention_bias_eff = α · attention_bias_base. When confidence is very low α→0 (falls back to identity-proxy behavior and disables strong control regularization); when confidence is high...

evaluation.fairness_protocol: Mostly adequate: you specify (i) same online interaction budgets, (ii) same generator as parent without CGLC/ATCG, (iii) CGLC with fixed α=1, plus step-wise MBRL baselines, and multi-seed reporting. evaluation.feedback: This operator is a classic 'add a gate' incremental tweak (mechanism-commit-innovation on top of CGLC) and does not add a new scientific mechanism beyond confidence-weighting; ICML bar risk: novelty is low unless you can (a) justify sv_gap→calibrated α as a principled uncertainty estimator for controllability regularization, and (b) demonstrate new failure science (when/why Gramian misestimation hurts, and how gating changes that). evaluation.defect_fix_summary: Addresses defects ['Gramian misestimation', 'sensitivity to Gramian-confidence thresholding', partially 'over-conservatism'] by replacing a brittle fixed-strength controllability prior with a smooth, learned confidence gate that downweights λ_ctrl and attention bias when Gramian confidence is low, reducing harmful misconditioning from noisy low-rank Gramian estimates.

iterations.rationale: Add an Adaptive-Temperature Confidence Gate (ATCG) that modulates (a) the control-energy regularizer weight λ_ctrl and (b) the CGLC attention-bias scale as a smooth function of the Gramian-confidence metric. ATCG outputs a scalar α ∈ [0,1] computed from the Gramian-confidence (sv_gap) via a small monotonic neural calibration curve (2-layer MLP with sigmoid), producing effective λ_ctrl_eff = α · λ_ctrl_base and attention_bias_eff = α · attention_bias_base. When confidence is very low α→0 (falls back to identity-proxy behavior and disables strong control regularization); when confidence is high...

evaluation.fairness_protocol: Mostly adequate: you specify (i) same online interaction budgets, (ii) same generator as parent without CGLC/ATCG, (iii) CGLC with fixed α=1, plus step-wise MBRL baselines, and multi-seed reporting. evaluation.feedback: This operator is a classic 'add a gate' incremental tweak (mechanism-commit-innovation on top of CGLC) and does not add a new scientific mechanism beyond confidence-weighting; ICML bar risk: novelty is low unless you can (a) justify sv_gap→calibrated α as a principled uncertainty estimator for controllability regularization, and (b) demonstrate new failure science (when/why Gramian misestimation hurts, and how gating changes that). evaluation.defect_fix_summary: Addresses defects ['Gramian misestimation', 'sensitivity to Gramian-confidence thresholding', partially 'over-conservatism'] by replacing a brittle fixed-strength controllability prior with a smooth, learned confidence gate that downweights λ_ctrl and attention bias when Gramian confidence is low, reducing harmful misconditioning from noisy low-rank Gramian estimates.

iterations.rationale: Add an Adaptive-Temperature Confidence Gate (ATCG) that modulates (a) the control-energy regularizer weight λ_ctrl and (b) the CGLC attention-bias scale as a smooth function of the Gramian-confidence metric. ATCG outputs a scalar α ∈ [0,1] computed from the Gramian-confidence (sv_gap) via a small monotonic neural calibration curve (2-layer MLP with sigmoid), producing effective λ_ctrl_eff = α · λ_ctrl_base and attention_bias_eff = α · attention_bias_base. When confidence is very low α→0 (falls back to identity-proxy behavior and disables strong control regularization); when confidence is high...

evaluation.fairness_protocol: Mostly adequate: you specify (i) same online interaction budgets, (ii) same generator as parent without CGLC/ATCG, (iii) CGLC with fixed α=1, plus step-wise MBRL baselines, and multi-seed reporting. evaluation.feedback: This operator is a classic 'add a gate' incremental tweak (mechanism-commit-innovation on top of CGLC) and does not add a new scientific mechanism beyond confidence-weighting; ICML bar risk: novelty is low unless you can (a) justify sv_gap→calibrated α as a principled uncertainty estimator for controllability regularization, and (b) demonstrate new failure science (when/why Gramian misestimation hurts, and how gating changes that). evaluation.defect_fix_summary: Addresses defects ['Gramian misestimation', 'sensitivity to Gramian-confidence thresholding', partially 'over-conservatism'] by replacing a brittle fixed-strength controllability prior with a smooth, learned confidence gate that downweights λ_ctrl and attention bias when Gramian confidence is low, reducing harmful misconditioning from noisy low-rank Gramian estimates.

iterations.rationale: Add an Adaptive-Temperature Confidence Gate (ATCG) that modulates (a) the control-energy regularizer weight λ_ctrl and (b) the CGLC attention-bias scale as a smooth function of the Gramian-confidence metric. ATCG outputs a scalar α ∈ [0,1] computed from the Gramian-confidence (sv_gap) via a small monotonic neural calibration curve (2-layer MLP with sigmoid), producing effective λ_ctrl_eff = α · λ_ctrl_base and attention_bias_eff = α · attention_bias_base. When confidence is very low α→0 (falls back to identity-proxy behavior and disables strong control regularization); when confidence is high...

evaluation.fairness_protocol: Mostly adequate: you specify (i) same online interaction budgets, (ii) same generator as parent without CGLC/ATCG, (iii) CGLC with fixed α=1, plus step-wise MBRL baselines, and multi-seed reporting. evaluation.feedback: This operator is a classic 'add a gate' incremental tweak (mechanism-commit-innovation on top of CGLC) and does not add a new scientific mechanism beyond confidence-weighting; ICML bar risk: novelty is low unless you can (a) justify sv_gap→calibrated α as a principled uncertainty estimator for controllability regularization, and (b) demonstrate new failure science (when/why Gramian misestimation hurts, and how gating changes that). evaluation.defect_fix_summary: Addresses defects ['Gramian misestimation', 'sensitivity to Gramian-confidence thresholding', partially 'over-conservatism'] by replacing a brittle fixed-strength controllability prior with a smooth, learned confidence gate that downweights λ_ctrl and attention bias when Gramian confidence is low, reducing harmful misconditioning from noisy low-rank Gramian estimates.

iterations.rationale: Add an Adaptive-Temperature Confidence Gate (ATCG) that modulates (a) the control-energy regularizer weight λ_ctrl and (b) the CGLC attention-bias scale as a smooth function of the Gramian-confidence metric. ATCG outputs a scalar α ∈ [0,1] computed from the Gramian-confidence (sv_gap) via a small monotonic neural calibration curve (2-layer MLP with sigmoid), producing effective λ_ctrl_eff = α · λ_ctrl_base and attention_bias_eff = α · attention_bias_base. When confidence is very low α→0 (falls back to identity-proxy behavior and disables strong control regularization); when confidence is high...

iterations.rationale: Add an Adaptive-Temperature Confidence Gate (ATCG) that modulates (a) the control-energy regularizer weight λ_ctrl and (b) the CGLC attention-bias scale as a smooth function of the Gramian-confidence metric. ATCG outputs a scalar α ∈ [0,1] computed from the Gramian-confidence (sv_gap) via a small monotonic neural calibration curve (2-layer MLP with sigmoid), producing effective λ_ctrl_eff = α · λ_ctrl_base and attention_bias_eff = α · attention_bias_base. idea.title: Controllability-Guided Flow Matching for Online MBRL (CtrlFlow + CGLC)
idea.abstract: Replace step-wise dynamics modeling in online model-based RL with a conditional flow-matching trajectory generator that directly samples full trajectories from initial states. Augment the conditional flow-matching generator with a Controllability-Guided Latent Conditioner (CGLC) which injects a minimal-control-energy scalar—computed from a low-rank empirical nonlinear controllability Gramian approximation—into the trajectory generator and as an attention bias. Train the flow-matching model with a combined loss: flow-matching likelihood, a minimum-control-energy regularizer (L_control) derived from the Gramian, and a value-guidance term to bias sampling toward high-return trajectories. The design preserves full-trajectory generation (no step-wise dynamics predictors), and targets improved ...

Mitigation: use small k and low-rank randomized SVD for efficiency, monitor singular-value gaps as approximation-confidence; fallback to an identity proxy when confidence is low; include Gramian-approximation ablation. 3) Compute overhead: periodic SVD and extra conditioning increase training cost.
